quiz_questions.question_text: What is the purpose of a data table in science?

options.text: To organize collected information in rows and columns for easy reading
To replace graphs entirely so that no graphing is ever needed
To display the hypothesis and conclusion of an experiment only
To show only the final answer without including any raw data values

quiz_questions.explanation: A data table organizes information into rows and columns so scientists can easily record, read, and compare measurements. Well-organized data tables are essential for accurate analysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What should be included in the column headers of a data table?

options.text: Only numbers without any labels or descriptions
The variable name and its units of measurement
Only the scientist's name and the date of testing
The conclusion of the experiment and hypothesis

quiz_questions.explanation: Column headers should include the variable name and its units (for example, 'Time (seconds)' or 'Mass (grams)'). This tells readers exactly what each column of data represents.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you calculate the mean (average) of a set of data?

options.text: Subtract the smallest value from the largest value in the set
Add all values together and divide by the number of values
Find the value that appears most often in the entire data set
Arrange the values in order and pick the one in the middle

quiz_questions.explanation: To find the mean, add all the values and divide by the total number of values. For example, the mean of 4, 6, and 8 is (4 + 6 + 8) ÷ 3 = 6.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the range of a data set?

options.text: The value that appears most frequently in the data
The difference between the largest and smallest values
The middle value when data is arranged in order
The sum of all values divided by the total count

quiz_questions.explanation: The range is calculated by subtracting the smallest value from the largest. For example, if your data is 3, 7, 9, 12, 15, then the range is 15 − 3 = 12. Range shows the spread of your data.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student records these test scores: 85, 90, 78, 92, 85. What is the mode?
quiz_questions.explanation: The mode is the value that appears most frequently. In this data set, 85 appears twice while all other values appear only once, making 85 the mode.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the median of the data set: 3, 7, 9, 12, 15?
quiz_questions.explanation: The median is the middle value when data is arranged in order. In the set 3, 7, 9, 12, 15, the middle value is 9 (with two values below and two above).

quiz_questions.question_text: Where should the independent variable go in a data table?

options.text: In the last column on the right side of the table
In the first column on the left side of the table
In the title row at the very top of the data table
It should not be included in the data table at all

quiz_questions.explanation: By convention, the independent variable is placed in the first (leftmost) column of a data table. The dependent variable and any calculated values go in the columns to the right.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student records the following masses: 12.3 g, 12.5 g, 12.4 g, 12.3 g, 12.5 g. What is the mean?
quiz_questions.explanation: Add all values: 12.3 + 12.5 + 12.4 + 12.3 + 12.5 = 62.0. Divide by 5: 62.0 ÷ 5 = 12.4 g. The mean mass is 12.4 grams.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why should a data table have a descriptive title?

options.text: So readers know what data the table contains and what experiment it relates to
To make the table look more decorative and visually appealing on the page
Because tables without titles will cause errors in all calculations performed
So the teacher knows which student submitted the data table for grading

quiz_questions.explanation: A descriptive title tells the reader what data is contained in the table and what experiment it came from. Without a title, the data lacks context and is much harder to interpret correctly.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the range of the data set: 14, 22, 18, 30, 25?
quiz_questions.explanation: The range is the largest value minus the smallest value: 30 − 14 = 16. This tells us the data spans 16 units from lowest to highest.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the mean (average) in data analysis?

options.text: The sum of all data values divided by the total number of values in the set
The middle number when all values are arranged from smallest to largest
The most frequently occurring value in an entire collected data set
The difference between the highest and lowest values in a data set

quiz_questions.explanation: The mean is calculated by adding all data values together and dividing by how many values there are. It gives a central value that represents the typical measurement in the data set.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the median in a data set?

options.text: The average of the highest and lowest values in the data
The middle value when all numbers are arranged in order
The value that appears the most times in a data set
The total of all values divided by their count in the set

quiz_questions.explanation: The median is found by arranging all values in order and selecting the middle one. If there is an even number of values, the median is the average of the two middle values.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the mode of a data set?

options.text: The value that occurs most frequently in the data set
The average calculated by adding and dividing all data values
The middle value found after arranging data in numerical order
The difference between the maximum and minimum data values

quiz_questions.explanation: The mode is the value that appears most often. A data set can have one mode, more than one mode (if multiple values tie for most frequent), or no mode at all (if all values appear the same number of times).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'qualitative data' mean?

options.text: Data described with words that relate to qualities like color, shape, or texture
Data that can be measured with numbers and recorded using specific units
Data that has been verified and confirmed by multiple scientists in the field
Data that is collected only from controlled experiments in laboratory settings

quiz_questions.explanation: Qualitative data describes qualities or characteristics that cannot be measured with numbers, such as color (red), texture (smooth), smell (sweet), or shape (round).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'quantitative data' mean?

options.text: Data that describes qualities using words such as color and texture
Data that can be measured and expressed using numbers and units
Data that only comes from surveys and questionnaires given to people
Data that changes constantly and cannot be recorded in any data table

quiz_questions.explanation: Quantitative data is numerical and can be measured, such as height (152 cm), mass (45 kg), temperature (22°C), or time (30 seconds). Think 'quantity' = numbers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'data analysis' mean?

options.text: The process of examining data to find patterns, trends, and relationships
The process of collecting data from an experiment before any calculations
The step of writing a hypothesis before conducting any experiment tests
The process of designing the experimental procedure and gathering tools

quiz_questions.explanation: Data analysis is the process of examining collected data to identify patterns, calculate statistics (mean, median, range), create graphs, and draw conclusions about the experiment's results.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'significant figures' mean in data recording?

options.text: The digits in a measurement that are known with certainty plus one estimated digit
The total number of decimal places written after the decimal point in a value
Only the digits that appear before the decimal point in a measured value
The largest and smallest values recorded in a complete data set overall

quiz_questions.explanation: Significant figures include all digits that are known with certainty from a measurement, plus one final estimated digit. They communicate the precision of a measurement.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'anomaly' in a data set?

options.text: A data point that is very close to the mean value of the data set
An unusual result that does not fit the pattern of the rest of the data
The most common measurement recorded during the experimental trials
The average of the first and last values in the entire collected data set

quiz_questions.explanation: An anomaly (or outlier) is a data point that deviates significantly from the overall pattern. It may result from a measurement error, equipment malfunction, or unusual experimental condition.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'precision' mean in data collection?

options.text: How close repeated measurements are to one another when taken multiple times
How close a measured value is to the actual or accepted true value overall
How many data points are collected during a single trial of an experiment
How quickly the scientist is able to record the data values during tests

quiz_questions.explanation: Precision describes how close repeated measurements are to each other. If you measure something five times and get 10.2, 10.3, 10.2, 10.3, 10.2, your measurements are very precise.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'accuracy' mean in data collection?

options.text: How close repeated measurements are to each other when taken multiple times
How close a measured value is to the true or accepted value of that quantity
How many digits are used to express a measurement in a data table entry
How many trials the scientist performed during the course of the experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: Accuracy describes how close a measurement is to the true or accepted value. A measurement can be precise (consistent) but not accurate (close to the true value), or vice versa.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the growth of plants over 5 weeks and records: 2 cm, 4 cm, 6 cm, 8 cm, 10 cm. What is the average growth per week?

options.text: 4 cm per week
6 cm per week
8 cm per week
5 cm per week

quiz_questions.explanation: Calculate the mean: (2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10) ÷ 5 = 30 ÷ 5 = 6 cm. The average growth per week is 6 cm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lab group records the time it takes a ball to roll down a ramp in 5 trials: 2.3 s, 2.5 s, 2.4 s, 5.1 s, 2.4 s. Which value is likely an anomaly?

options.text: 2.3 seconds
2.5 seconds
5.1 seconds
2.4 seconds

quiz_questions.explanation: The value 5.1 seconds is much higher than the other four values, which cluster around 2.3–2.5 seconds. This makes it a likely anomaly (outlier), possibly caused by the ball being obstructed or a timing error.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student records the number of sit-ups done by classmates: 15, 22, 18, 20, 22, 17, 22, 19. Her teacher asks for the mode. What should she report?

options.text: 19.4 sit-ups
22 sit-ups
19 sit-ups
18 sit-ups

quiz_questions.explanation: The mode is the most frequently occurring value. The number 22 appears three times, more than any other value, making it the mode of this data set.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist measures the temperature of a solution every 10 minutes: 25°C, 30°C, 35°C, 40°C, 45°C. She notices the data shows a pattern. What pattern does the data show?

options.text: The temperature increases by 5°C every 10 minutes at a constant rate
The temperature decreases by 5°C every 10 minutes at a constant rate
The temperature stays the same and does not change at all over time
The temperature changes randomly with no predictable pattern at all

quiz_questions.explanation: The data shows a consistent increase of 5°C every 10 minutes (25→30→35→40→45). This is a linear pattern — the temperature rises at a steady, constant rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student observes a rock and writes: 'The rock is gray, rough, and has shiny flecks.' What type of data is this?

options.text: Quantitative data based on numerical measurements
Qualitative data based on observed qualities and descriptions
Experimental data recorded from a controlled laboratory test
Calculated data derived from mathematical formulas and equations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is qualitative data because it describes qualities (color, texture, appearance) using words rather than numbers. Quantitative data would include measurements like 'The rock has a mass of 45 grams.'

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the length of a pencil five times: 18.2 cm, 18.3 cm, 18.2 cm, 18.2 cm, 18.3 cm. The actual length is 19.0 cm. Are the measurements precise, accurate, or both?

options.text: Both precise and accurate because the values are consistent and correct
Accurate but not precise because they are close to the true value only
Precise but not accurate because they are consistent but far from the true value
Neither precise nor accurate because the values are all different from each other

quiz_questions.explanation: The measurements are precise because they are very close to each other (18.2–18.3 cm). However, they are not accurate because they are all far from the true value of 19.0 cm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A class collects data on how many books each student read last month: 2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3, 2, 5, 2, 3. The teacher asks for the median. What is it?

options.text: 3 books
2 books
2.5 books
2.7 books

quiz_questions.explanation: First arrange in order: 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 5. With 10 values, the median is the average of the 5th and 6th values: (2 + 3) ÷ 2 = 2.5 books.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist collects temperature data for a week but notices one day's reading is 150°C when all other days are between 20°C and 25°C. What should she do with this unusual reading?

options.text: Include it without question because all data should always be included
Investigate the reading for possible errors before deciding to include it or not
Change it to 25°C so that it fits with the rest of the data in the set
Delete all data from the week and restart the entire data collection process

quiz_questions.explanation: The 150°C reading is likely an error (perhaps the thermometer malfunctioned or was misread). The scientist should investigate the cause. If it was a confirmed error, it can be noted and excluded from analysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student's data table has no units in the column headers. Her temperatures read 22, 25, 28, 31. A classmate asks if those are in Celsius or Fahrenheit. Why does the missing information matter?

options.text: It does not matter because temperature is always measured in Celsius in science
Without units, the data cannot be properly interpreted or compared with other data
It only matters if the student plans to convert the temperature to another unit
Missing units automatically make all the numerical values recorded incorrect

quiz_questions.explanation: Without units, the data is ambiguous. 22°C and 22°F are very different temperatures (22°C ≈ 72°F). Units are essential for proper interpretation, comparison, and reproducibility of data.

quiz_questions.question_text: A group of students measures the mass of a marble three times: Trial 1 = 5.2 g, Trial 2 = 5.3 g, Trial 3 = 5.1 g. Their teacher says the accepted mass is 5.2 g. Are their results accurate, precise, or both?

options.text: Only accurate because the average matches the accepted value of 5.2 grams
Only precise because the three measurements are very close to one another
Both accurate and precise because the values are close to each other and to 5.2 g
Neither accurate nor precise because none of the individual trials exactly equals 5.2 g

quiz_questions.explanation: The measurements are precise (they cluster tightly between 5.1 and 5.3 g) and accurate (their average of 5.2 g matches the accepted value). This is the ideal result in data collection.
